1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain model for the digital wallet / payment system.
3	
4	The "nouns" of the system live here: enums, the typed exception family,
5	the immutable value records (LedgerEntry, TransferResult), and the one
6	mutable entity that owns its own money rule (Wallet).
7	"""
8	
9	from dataclasses import dataclass
10	from enum import Enum
11	
12	
13	# ── WHY THIS EXCEPTION FAMILY ────────────────────────────────────────────────
14	# Every failure mode in this domain is its own typed error, all sharing ONE
15	# base. That lets callers catch "anything wallet-related" with WalletError, OR
16	# catch the exact case (e.g. only InsufficientFundsError) when they care.
17	# 🎙️ "I never raise a bare ValueError from my own code — every failure is a
18	#     named WalletError subclass, so a caller reads the failure mode from the type."
19	class WalletError(Exception):
20	    """Base class for every error this system raises."""
21	
22	
23	class UnknownWalletError(WalletError):
24	    """A wallet id was referenced that the system has never created."""
25	
26	
27	class WalletAlreadyExistsError(WalletError):
28	    """create_wallet was called with an id that already exists."""
29	
30	
31	class InvalidAmountError(WalletError):
32	    """An amount was zero or negative (money moves are strictly positive)."""
33	
34	
35	class InsufficientFundsError(WalletError):
36	    """A debit would drive a wallet balance below zero."""
37	
38	
39	class SameWalletTransferError(WalletError):
40	    """A transfer named the same wallet as both source and destination."""
41	
42	
43	class IdempotencyConflictError(WalletError):
44	    """An idempotency key was reused with DIFFERENT transfer parameters.
45	
46	    A retry must repeat the SAME (src, dst, amount); reusing the key for a
47	    different transfer is a client bug, not a safe retry — we reject it loudly.
48	    """
49	
50	
51	class EntryType(Enum):
52	    """Which side of the double-entry ledger a row sits on.
53	
54	    DEBIT removes money from a wallet (negative delta); CREDIT adds money
55	    to a wallet (positive delta). Every transfer writes exactly one of each.
56	    """
57	
58	    DEBIT = "DEBIT"
59	    CREDIT = "CREDIT"
60	
61	
62	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (LedgerEntry) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
63	# A ledger entry is a permanent, never-edited fact: "wallet W changed by D
64	# cents in transfer T". It is frozen because the books are append-only — you
65	# never rewrite history, you only add correcting entries.
66	# 🎙️ "Each transfer writes two of these — a debit and a credit — and their
67	#     deltas sum to zero. That's how I prove money is conserved."
68	@dataclass(frozen=True)
69	class LedgerEntry:
70	    """One immutable row of the double-entry ledger."""
71	
72	    transfer_id: str
73	    wallet_id: str
74	    entry_type: EntryType
75	    # WHY: signed cents. DEBIT stores a negative delta, CREDIT a positive one,
76	    #      so summing the whole ledger must yield exactly 0 if the books balance.
77	    delta_cents: int
78	
79	
80	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (TransferResult) ──────────────────────────────────────────
81	# The value a transfer returns. It is frozen so a caller can cache it and the
82	# idempotency store can hand back the SAME object on a retry without anyone
83	# being able to mutate the recorded outcome.
84	# 🎙️ "On a retried transfer I return this exact recorded result instead of
85	#     moving money again — that's what 'applied exactly once' means."
86	@dataclass(frozen=True)
87	class TransferResult:
88	    """The recorded outcome of one applied transfer."""
89	
90	    transfer_id: str
91	    idempotency_key: str
92	    source_wallet_id: str
93	    dest_wallet_id: str
94	    amount_cents: int
95	    source_balance_after: int
96	    dest_balance_after: int
97	
98	
99	# ── WHY THIS CLASS (Wallet) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
100	# The Wallet is the Information Expert for its own money: it is the ONLY thing
101	# allowed to change its balance, and it refuses to go negative. The service
102	# never writes wallet.balance directly — it asks the wallet to credit/debit.
103	# 🎙️ "Information Expert — the wallet owns its balance and guards the
104	#     no-negative rule itself, so that rule can't be forgotten in some code path."
105	@dataclass
106	class Wallet:
107	    """A user's money holder. Balance is integer cents, never a float."""
108	
109	    wallet_id: str
110	    balance_cents: int = 0
111	
112	    def credit(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:
113	        """Add money. Used by the CREDIT side of a transfer and by deposit."""
114	        # WHY: validate at the boundary — a zero or negative credit is a bug,
115	        #      not a no-op, so we reject it with a typed error before mutating.
116	        if amount_cents <= 0:
117	            raise InvalidAmountError(f"credit amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")
118	
119	        self.balance_cents += amount_cents
120	
121	    def debit(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:
122	        """Remove money, refusing to go negative. The core money invariant."""
123	        if amount_cents <= 0:
124	            raise InvalidAmountError(f"debit amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")
125	
126	        # WHY: this single check is the no-negative rule. Because only the
127	        #      wallet can debit itself, there is exactly ONE place this rule
128	        #      lives — it can never be bypassed by a service code path.
129	        if amount_cents > self.balance_cents:
130	            raise InsufficientFundsError(
131	                f"wallet {self.wallet_id} has {self.balance_cents} cents, "
132	                f"cannot debit {amount_cents}"
133	            )
134	
135	        self.balance_cents -= amount_cents
136

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The orchestrator: a digital wallet service with a double-entry ledger and
3	idempotent, atomic transfers.
4	
5	The clean WalletService is single-threaded-correct and has ZERO lock code in
6	its method bodies — the only concurrency seam is a no-op `_lock_wallets`
7	context manager. The ThreadSafeWalletService subclass overrides ONLY that hook
8	to take per-wallet locks in a deadlock-free global order. Nothing else changes.
9	"""
10	
11	import threading
12	from collections import defaultdict
13	from contextlib import contextmanager
14	from typing import Iterator
15	
16	from gateways import Clock, IdGenerator, SequentialIdGenerator, SystemClock
17	from models import (
18	    EntryType,
19	    IdempotencyConflictError,
20	    InvalidAmountError,
21	    LedgerEntry,
22	    SameWalletTransferError,
23	    TransferResult,
24	    UnknownWalletError,
25	    Wallet,
26	    WalletAlreadyExistsError,
27	)
28	
29	
30	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
31	# The WalletService is the brain. It owns every wallet, the append-only ledger,
32	# and the idempotency store (key -> the result we already produced). Money only
33	# ever moves through here, so it is the one place that guarantees: each transfer
34	# writes two ledger rows summing to zero, retries apply once, and balances are
35	# never negative.
36	# 🎙️ "Everything flows through the service: it holds the wallets, the ledger,
37	#     and the idempotency map. The wallets guard their own balances; the service
38	#     guards atomicity and idempotency."
39	class WalletService:
40	    """Single-threaded-correct core. No locks in any method body."""
41	
42	    def __init__(
43	        self,
44	        clock: Clock | None = None,
45	        id_gen: IdGenerator | None = None,
46	    ) -> None:
47	        self._wallets: dict[str, Wallet] = {}
48	        # WHY: the ledger is append-only — we never edit or delete a row, we
49	        #      only add. Summing every delta must always equal 0 (books balance).
50	        self._ledger: list[LedgerEntry] = []
51	        # WHY: idempotency store. Maps an idempotency key to the result we
52	        #      already committed for it, so a retry returns that SAME result
53	        #      instead of moving money a second time.
54	        self._results_by_key: dict[str, TransferResult] = {}
55	        self._clock: Clock = clock if clock is not None else SystemClock()
56	        self._id_gen: IdGenerator = id_gen if id_gen is not None else SequentialIdGenerator()
57	
58	    # ── concurrency hook (no-op in the clean core) ──────────────────────────
59	    # ── WHY THIS METHOD ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
60	    # This does NOTHING in the clean version — it just yields. The thread-safe
61	    # subclass overrides it to acquire the two wallet locks in a fixed order.
62	    # Keeping it here means the core method bodies contain zero lock code; the
63	    # whole thread-safety story is one overridden method.
64	    # 🎙️ "My core has no lock imports in its logic. The thread-safe version is a
65	    #     subclass that overrides only this one hook — the diff is tiny."
66	    @contextmanager
67	    def _lock_wallets(self, first: Wallet, second: Wallet) -> Iterator[None]:
68	        yield
69	
70	    # ── account management ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
71	    def create_wallet(self, wallet_id: str, initial_cents: int = 0) -> Wallet:
72	        """Register a new wallet, optionally pre-funded. Public API."""
73	        if wallet_id in self._wallets:
74	            # WHY: re-creating an existing id would silently wipe a balance.
75	            #      We refuse it as a typed error rather than overwrite money.
76	            raise WalletAlreadyExistsError(f"wallet {wallet_id} already exists")
77	
78	        if initial_cents < 0:
79	            raise InvalidAmountError(f"initial balance cannot be negative: {initial_cents}")
80	
81	        wallet = Wallet(wallet_id=wallet_id, balance_cents=initial_cents)
82	        self._wallets[wallet_id] = wallet
83	        return wallet
84	
85	    def balance_of(self, wallet_id: str) -> int:
86	        """Return a wallet's current balance in cents. Public API."""
87	        return self._get(wallet_id).balance_cents
88	
89	    # ── single-wallet money moves ───────────────────────────────────────────
90	    def deposit(self, wallet_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> int:
91	        """Add external money into a wallet. Returns the new balance.
92	
93	        A deposit is a single credit. We still record a ledger entry so the
94	        books show where the money came from, paired with an external sink.
95	        """
96	        wallet = self._get(wallet_id)
97	
98	        with self._lock_wallets(wallet, wallet):
99	            transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
100	            wallet.credit(amount_cents)
101	            # WHY: even a deposit is double-entry. The wallet is CREDITED and an
102	            #      EXTERNAL source is DEBITED, so the two deltas still sum to 0
103	            #      and the whole-ledger sum stays at exactly 0.
104	            self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id="EXTERNAL", dst_id=wallet_id, amount=amount_cents)
105	
106	        return wallet.balance_cents
107	
108	    def withdraw(self, wallet_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> int:
109	        """Remove money out of a wallet to the external world. Returns balance."""
110	        wallet = self._get(wallet_id)
111	
112	        with self._lock_wallets(wallet, wallet):
113	            transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
114	            # WHY: debit FIRST inside the guard. If the wallet is short it raises
115	            #      InsufficientFundsError and we never touch the ledger — no
116	            #      half-written transfer.
117	            wallet.debit(amount_cents)
118	            self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id=wallet_id, dst_id="EXTERNAL", amount=amount_cents)
119	
120	        return wallet.balance_cents
121	
122	    # ── the interesting one: atomic + idempotent transfer ───────────────────
123	    def transfer(
124	        self,
125	        source_id: str,
126	        dest_id: str,
127	        amount_cents: int,
128	        idempotency_key: str,
129	    ) -> TransferResult:
130	        """Move money between two wallets — atomic and idempotent.
131	
132	        Atomic: either both wallets change and two ledger rows are written, or
133	        nothing changes. Idempotent: a retry with the same key returns the
134	        original result and moves no further money.
135	        """
136	        # WHY: validate the SHAPE of the request before touching any state —
137	        #      unknown wallets, self-transfer, non-positive amount all reject here.
138	        self._validate_transfer(source_id, dest_id, amount_cents)
139	        source = self._get(source_id)
140	        dest = self._get(dest_id)
141	
142	        with self._lock_wallets(source, dest):
143	            # WHY: the idempotency check is INSIDE the lock so two concurrent
144	            #      retries of the same key can't both pass the check and both
145	            #      apply. The first commits and records; the second sees the
146	            #      recorded result and returns it unchanged.
147	            replay = self._replay_if_seen(idempotency_key, source_id, dest_id, amount_cents)
148	
149	            if replay is not None:
150	                return replay
151	
152	            transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
153	            # WHY: debit BEFORE credit. If the source is short, debit raises and
154	            #      nothing else has happened — no credit, no ledger rows, no
155	            #      idempotency record. The transfer is all-or-nothing.
156	            source.debit(amount_cents)
157	            dest.credit(amount_cents)
158	            self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id=source_id, dst_id=dest_id, amount=amount_cents)
159	
160	            result = TransferResult(
161	                transfer_id=transfer_id,
162	                idempotency_key=idempotency_key,
163	                source_wallet_id=source_id,
164	                dest_wallet_id=dest_id,
165	                amount_cents=amount_cents,
166	                source_balance_after=source.balance_cents,
167	                dest_balance_after=dest.balance_cents,
168	            )
169	            # WHY: record the result keyed by the idempotency key INSIDE the lock
170	            #      so it is published atomically with the money move. A retry that
171	            #      arrives next will find it.
172	            self._results_by_key[idempotency_key] = result
173	            return result
174	
175	    # ── ledger queries (for proving the books balance) ──────────────────────
176	    def ledger_balance(self) -> int:
177	        """Sum of every ledger delta. Must always be 0. Public API."""
178	        # WHY: this is the system-wide conservation proof. Two-sided entries
179	        #      mean every cents moved appears once positive and once negative.
180	        return sum(entry.delta_cents for entry in self._ledger)
181	
182	    def total_wallet_balance(self) -> int:
183	        """Sum of all wallet balances — what users collectively hold."""
184	        return sum(w.balance_cents for w in self._wallets.values())
185	
186	    def ledger_entries(self) -> list[LedgerEntry]:
187	        """A read-only copy of the ledger. Public API."""
188	        # WHY: hand back a copy so a caller can't mutate our append-only log.
189	        return list(self._ledger)
190	
191	    # ── private helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
192	    def _get(self, wallet_id: str) -> Wallet:
193	        """Look up a wallet or raise the typed unknown-wallet error."""
194	        wallet = self._wallets.get(wallet_id)
195	
196	        if wallet is None:
197	            raise UnknownWalletError(f"no wallet with id {wallet_id}")
198	
199	        return wallet
200	
201	    def _validate_transfer(self, source_id: str, dest_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> None:
202	        """Guard the transfer boundary: known wallets, distinct, positive amount."""
203	        if source_id == dest_id:
204	            raise SameWalletTransferError(f"cannot transfer from {source_id} to itself")
205	
206	        if amount_cents <= 0:
207	            raise InvalidAmountError(f"transfer amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")
208	
209	        # WHY: confirm both wallets exist before locking; _get raises if not.
210	        self._get(source_id)
211	        self._get(dest_id)
212	
213	    def _replay_if_seen(
214	        self,
215	        idempotency_key: str,
216	        source_id: str,
217	        dest_id: str,
218	        amount_cents: int,
219	    ) -> TransferResult | None:
220	        """Return the recorded result for a seen key, or None for a new key.
221	
222	        If the key was seen but with DIFFERENT parameters, that's a client bug
223	        (a key reused for a different transfer) and we reject it loudly.
224	        """
225	        prior = self._results_by_key.get(idempotency_key)
226	
227	        if prior is None:
228	            return None
229	
230	        # WHY: a safe retry must repeat the SAME transfer. If the same key now
231	        #      carries a different (src, dst, amount), returning the old result
232	        #      would silently swallow the new intent — so we raise instead.
233	        same = (
234	            prior.source_wallet_id == source_id
235	            and prior.dest_wallet_id == dest_id
236	            and prior.amount_cents == amount_cents
237	        )
238	
239	        if not same:
240	            raise IdempotencyConflictError(
241	                f"idempotency key {idempotency_key} was used for a different transfer"
242	            )
243	
244	        return prior
245	
246	    def _record_pair(self, transfer_id: str, src_id: str, dst_id: str, amount: int) -> None:
247	        """Append the two ledger rows for one money move: a debit and a credit.
248	
249	        The two deltas are -amount and +amount, so they sum to zero. This is the
250	        single place ledger rows are created, so the books can't drift.
251	        """
252	        self._ledger.append(
253	            LedgerEntry(
254	                transfer_id=transfer_id,
255	                wallet_id=src_id,
256	                entry_type=EntryType.DEBIT,
257	                delta_cents=-amount,
258	            )
259	        )
260	        self._ledger.append(
261	            LedgerEntry(
262	                transfer_id=transfer_id,
263	                wallet_id=dst_id,
264	                entry_type=EntryType.CREDIT,
265	                delta_cents=+amount,
266	            )
267	        )
268	
269	
270	# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
271	# This is the §9 "second version". It changes NOTHING about the money logic —
272	# it overrides ONLY the _lock_wallets hook. Each wallet gets its own RLock, and
273	# we acquire the two locks in a fixed GLOBAL ORDER (sorted by wallet id) so two
274	# reciprocal transfers (A->B and B->A) can never deadlock by grabbing locks in
275	# opposite orders.
276	# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version is a subclass overriding one method. Per-wallet
277	#     RLocks acquired in wallet-id order — that ordered acquisition is what makes
278	#     it deadlock-free even when two transfers cross each other."
279	class ThreadSafeWalletService(WalletService):
280	    """Adds per-wallet locking with deadlock-free ordered acquisition."""
281	
282	    def __init__(
283	        self,
284	        clock: Clock | None = None,
285	        id_gen: IdGenerator | None = None,
286	    ) -> None:
287	        super().__init__(clock=clock, id_gen=id_gen)
288	        # WHY: one lock object PER wallet id, created on demand. RLock (reentrant)
289	        #      because a transfer that locks both wallets re-enters helpers that
290	        #      assume the lock is held; same thread re-acquiring is safe.
291	        self._wallet_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = defaultdict(threading.RLock)
292	        # WHY: a guard around the dict of locks itself. Two threads creating the
293	        #      same new wallet-lock key at once could otherwise race the dict.
294	        self._locks_guard = threading.Lock()
295	        # WHY: idempotency-store reads/writes and ledger appends also need
296	        #      protection when the two wallets differ; this one lock makes the
297	        #      shared registries atomic without affecting per-wallet parallelism.
298	        self._registry_lock = threading.RLock()
299	
300	    def _lock_for(self, wallet_id: str) -> threading.RLock:
301	        # WHY: lock the dict of locks first, then fetch the per-wallet lock, so
302	        #      two threads adding the same key don't corrupt the dict.
303	        with self._locks_guard:
304	            return self._wallet_locks[wallet_id]
305	
306	    @contextmanager
307	    def _lock_wallets(self, first: Wallet, second: Wallet) -> Iterator[None]:
308	        # WHY: acquire in a CONSISTENT GLOBAL ORDER (by wallet id). If transfer
309	        #      A->B grabs A then B, and B->A also grabs A then B (not B then A),
310	        #      neither can hold one lock while waiting for the other in reverse —
311	        #      that lock-ordering cycle is the only way a deadlock could form.
312	        ids = sorted({first.wallet_id, second.wallet_id})
313	        locks = [self._lock_for(wid) for wid in ids]
314	
315	        acquired: list[threading.RLock] = []
316	
317	        try:
318	            for lock in locks:
319	                lock.acquire()
320	                acquired.append(lock)
321	            # WHY: the registry lock guards the ledger + idempotency store so the
322	            #      two-sided write and the result publish are atomic too.
323	            with self._registry_lock:
324	                yield
325	        finally:
326	            # WHY: release in reverse acquisition order on EVERY path — including
327	            #      an exception mid-transfer — so a failed transfer never leaks
328	            #      a held lock.
329	            for lock in reversed(acquired):
330	                lock.release()
331

2	"""External dependencies the service injects, so tests stay deterministic.
3	
4	The service never calls `datetime.now()` or generates a random id inline.
5	It asks an injected Clock and IdGenerator. In tests we pass fakes so transfer
6	ids and timestamps are predictable and assertions read cleanly.

10	import threading
11	from datetime import datetime, timezone
12	from typing import Protocol
13	
14	
15	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE (Clock) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
16	# Time is injected, not read inline. A ledger entry could carry a timestamp,
17	# and a test must control "now" without sleeping. Passing a Clock makes that
18	# trivial — the fake clock just returns whatever instant the test sets.
19	# 🎙️ "I inject the clock so I can stamp ledger entries deterministically in
20	#     tests — no real wall-clock, no flaky time-based assertions."
21	class Clock(Protocol):
22	    def now(self) -> datetime: ...
23	
24	
25	class SystemClock:
26	    """Real clock — used by main.py and production."""
27	
28	    def now(self) -> datetime:
29	        return datetime.now(timezone.utc)
30	
31	
32	class FixedClock:
33	    """Test clock — returns a controllable instant."""
34	
35	    def __init__(self, start: datetime) -> None:
36	        self._t = start
37	
38	    def now(self) -> datetime:
39	        return self._t
40	
41	    def advance(self, seconds: float) -> None:
42	        # WHY: tests move time forward explicitly instead of sleeping.
43	        from datetime import timedelta
44	
45	        self._t = self._t + timedelta(seconds=seconds)
46	
47	
48	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE (IdGenerator) ─────────────────────────────────────────
49	# Transfer ids are generated through an injected source so tests get readable,
50	# numbered ids (transfer-1, transfer-2) instead of random UUIDs. Production
51	# swaps in a UUID generator. The generator is thread-safe because many threads
52	# may mint ids at once during a concurrent burst.
53	# 🎙️ "Numbered ids in tests make assertions readable; production uses UUIDs.
54	#     The counter is lock-guarded because concurrent transfers all mint ids."
55	class IdGenerator(Protocol):
56	    def next_id(self) -> str: ...
57	
58	
59	class SequentialIdGenerator:
60	    """Hands out transfer-1, transfer-2, … — readable and thread-safe."""
61	
62	    def __init__(self, prefix: str = "transfer") -> None:
63	        self._prefix = prefix
64	        self._counter = itertools.count(1)
65	        # WHY: itertools.count is NOT thread-safe across the GIL boundary for
66	        #      our purposes once we also format; a tiny lock makes next_id atomic
67	        #      so two threads never collide on the same id.
68	        self._lock = threading.Lock()
69	
70	    def next_id(self) -> str:
71	        with self._lock:
72	            n = next(self._counter)
73	
74	        return f"{self._prefix}-{n}"
75

197	def test_concurrent_reciprocal_and_duplicate_transfers_conserve_money() -> None:
198	    """N threads fire reciprocal (A<->B) plus duplicate-key transfers at one
199	    instant. We assert the FULL post-state, not just a count:
200	      - total money across all wallets == initial sum (conservation),
201	      - no wallet balance is negative,
202	      - duplicate-key transfers applied exactly once,
203	      - every ledger pair sums to zero (and the whole ledger sums to zero),
204	      - no deadlock (the join() returns within the timeout).
205	    """
206	    clock = FixedClock(datetime(2026, 6, 21, tzinfo=timezone.utc))
207	    svc = ThreadSafeWalletService(clock=clock, id_gen=SequentialIdGenerator())
208	
209	    start_a, start_b = 1_000_00, 1_000_00
210	    svc.create_wallet("A", initial_cents=start_a)
211	    svc.create_wallet("B", initial_cents=start_b)
212	    initial_total = start_a + start_b
213	
214	    n_pairs = 60
215	    amount = 100  # 1 dollar each direction
216	    # Two threads per pair share ONE duplicate key, so each duplicate must apply
217	    # exactly once no matter which of the two threads wins the race.
218	    dup_keys = [f"dup-{i}" for i in range(n_pairs)]

220	    # Total threads: each pair contributes an A->B (x2 duplicate) and a B->A.
221	    n_threads = n_pairs * 3
222	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
223	    errors: list[BaseException] = []
224	    lock = threading.Lock()
225	
226	    def fire(src: str, dst: str, key: str) -> None:
227	        barrier.wait()  # all threads hit the critical section at one instant

217	    # _record_pair(transfer_id, src_id, dst_id, amount)
218	```
219	
220	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
221	- **Why does the idempotency check live inside the lock?** So two concurrent retries of the same key can't both pass the check and both apply. The first commits and records; the second sees the recorded result.
222	- **Why is `_lock_wallets` a no-op in the core?** It keeps the clean version free of any lock code — the entire thread-safety story is one overridden method in the subclass.
223	- **Why return a `TransferResult` and not just the balances?** A retry must hand back the *exact original outcome*; an immutable result object is the natural thing to cache by key.

424	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
425	
426	### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)
427	
428	> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `Wallet` owns its balance AND the no-negative rule, because it's the thing that has the balance — so that rule lives in exactly one place and can't be bypassed by a service code path. `WalletService` owns allocation across wallets, the ledger, and the idempotency store, because it's the only thing that sees *all* wallets and *all* transfers — no single wallet could maintain a system-wide conservation invariant. `LedgerEntry` owns nothing but the truth of one money move, frozen forever."
429	
430	### 5.2 Applied patterns
431	
432	**Template Method — the `_lock_wallets` hook**
433	- **Why it fits HERE:** the *only* thing that differs between the clean core and the thread-safe version is *how the two wallets are locked*. By making locking a hook method that's a no-op in the base, the entire money logic is written once; the subclass overrides one method.
434	- **Interface + concrete impl:** `WalletService._lock_wallets()` (yields, does nothing) → `ThreadSafeWalletService._lock_wallets()` (acquires per-wallet RLocks in id order).
435	- 🎙️ **Script:** "My core has zero lock code in its method bodies. The thread-safe version is a subclass overriding one hook — `_lock_wallets`. The diff is about a dozen lines."
436	- 💬 **Layman gloss:** "The core doesn't know anything about locks — it just calls a 'do the locking' step that does nothing. The thread-safe version swaps in a real lock for that one step."
437	
438	**Idempotency store (Memoization for side-effecting ops)**
439	- **Why it fits HERE:** money moves are *not* naturally repeatable — running a transfer twice moves money twice. By recording the outcome keyed by the client's idempotency key, a retry becomes a cache hit that returns the original result and moves nothing.
440	- **Interface + concrete impl:** `_results_by_key: dict[str, TransferResult]`, consulted by `_replay_if_seen` before any money moves.
441	- 🎙️ **Script:** "Idempotency is just a map from the client's key to the result I already produced. Before moving money I check that map; if the key's there with the same params, I return the recorded result."
442	- 💬 **Layman gloss:** "It's like a receipt drawer — if you hand me the same order number again, I show you the receipt instead of charging you a second time."
443	
444	### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)
445	
446	- **Single Responsibility:** `Wallet` owns its balance, `WalletService` owns coordination + the ledger, `LedgerEntry` is an inert fact. Three reasons to change, three classes.
447	- **Open/Closed:** adding thread-safety = a new subclass overriding `_lock_wallets`, zero edits to the core. A new money move (e.g. a fee) = a new method using the same `_record_pair` helper.
448	- **Liskov Substitution:** `ThreadSafeWalletService` is a drop-in for `WalletService` — same public contract, same return types. Tests run the core against the base and the race test against the subclass.
449	- **Dependency Inversion:** `WalletService` depends on the `Clock`/`IdGenerator` *protocols*, never on `SystemClock`/`SequentialIdGenerator` concretely — tests inject fakes.
450	
451	### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
452	
453	- **Template Method** — real: `_lock_wallets` is a `@contextmanager` that yields in the base and is overridden in the subclass. Visible in §6/§9 code.
454	- **Idempotency store** — real: `_results_by_key` + `_replay_if_seen` are exercised by `test_duplicate_key_applies_once` and the concurrency test.
455	- I did **not** reach for a GoF Strategy here — there's no swappable *policy* in v1 (no fee rules, no allocation choice). If we needed pluggable fees, I'd introduce a `FeePolicy` Strategy at the `transfer` boundary; right now that would be an unused seam, so I left it out. Naming a pattern I didn't wire in would be a yellow flag.
456

459	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
460	
461	### A. The Key Insight
462	
463	> **Insight:** Every money move is **two ledger rows whose signed deltas sum to zero**, written together; and a transfer is made retry-safe by **recording its outcome under the client's idempotency key before returning** — so a retry is a cache hit, not a second move. For concurrency, the two-account lock is taken in a **fixed global order by wallet id**, which makes deadlock impossible by construction.
464	
465	> **Why it works:** Money is a *conserved* quantity — it only ever moves *between* holders, never appears or vanishes. Double-entry captures that exactly: debit one side, credit the other by the same amount, and the sum over the whole ledger is invariantly zero. Idempotency works because a committed transfer has a single canonical outcome; once recorded, the safe answer to "do it again" is "here's what already happened." And a deadlock needs two locks acquired in *opposite* orders by two threads — sorting the lock-acquisition order removes the cycle.
466	
467	> **What breaks without it:** A junior writes `src.balance -= amt; dst.balance += amt` with no ledger — now there's no way to *prove* the books balance, and a crash between the two lines silently destroys money. Without the idempotency store, a client retry on a flaky network double-charges. And locking "source then destination" in argument order means thread 1 doing A→B locks A then waits for B, while thread 2 doing B→A locks B then waits for A — classic deadlock, the process hangs forever. (I verified this: a deliberately-unordered variant of my own service hangs under the same test that my ordered version passes.)
468	
469	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
470	
471	#### `transfer` (the pipeline + the race-prone method)
472	
473	**(1) Pseudocode**
474	```
475	1. validate: source != dest, amount > 0, both wallets exist  (else typed error)
476	2. look up source and dest Wallet objects
477	3. ENTER critical section: lock both wallets in wallet-id order
478	4.   if idempotency_key already recorded:
479	5.       if recorded params == (source, dest, amount): return recorded result
480	6.       else: raise IdempotencyConflictError
481	7.   mint transfer_id
482	8.   source.debit(amount)          # raises InsufficientFundsError if short -> nothing else changed
483	9.   dest.credit(amount)
484	10.  append two ledger rows: (source, -amount), (dest, +amount)
485	11.  build TransferResult, record it under idempotency_key
486	12.  return result
487	13. EXIT critical section
488	```
489	
490	**(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script** — *This method has the check-then-act race. Steps 3–11 are the critical section.* "Step 1 validates the shape outside the lock — those reads don't change. Step 3 enters the lock. The non-obvious bit is step 4: I check idempotency *inside* the lock, so two concurrent retries of the same key can't both pass — the first commits at step 11, the second hits step 5 and returns the recorded result. Step 8 debits *first*: if the source is short it raises right there and steps 9–11 never run, so the transfer is all-or-nothing. Step 10 is where double-entry fires — two rows, signed deltas summing to zero."
491	
492	**(3) DRY RUN — `transfer("alice", "bob", 3000, key="t1")`** with `alice=10000, bob=0`:
493	
494	| Step | Action | State change |
495	|---|---|---|
496	| 1 | validate | alice≠bob ✓, 3000>0 ✓, both exist ✓ |
497	| 3 | lock wallets in order | sorted ids: `["alice","bob"]` → lock alice, then bob |
498	| 4 | idempotency check | `t1` not in `_results_by_key` → proceed |
499	| 7 | mint id | `transfer_id = "transfer-1"` |
500	| 8 | `alice.debit(3000)` | alice: 10000 → **7000** |
501	| 9 | `bob.credit(3000)` | bob: 0 → **3000** |
502	| 10 | append two rows | `_ledger += [(alice,-3000),(bob,+3000)]` → ledger sum = 0 |
503	| 11 | record result | `_results_by_key["t1"] = TransferResult(..., 7000, 3000)` |
504	| 12 | return | caller gets `TransferResult` with balances 7000/3000 |
505	
506	> *Edge probe: a second thread fires the same `key="t1"` while step 8 is mid-flight?* *It blocks at step 3 on alice's lock. When the first thread exits, the second enters, hits step 4, finds `t1` recorded, and returns the recorded result at step 5 — money moved exactly once.*
507	
508	#### `deposit` / `withdraw` (single-wallet moves)
509	
510	**Pseudocode (`withdraw`)**
511	```
512	1. look up wallet (else UnknownWalletError)
513	2. ENTER critical section (lock the one wallet)
514	3.   mint transfer_id
515	4.   wallet.debit(amount)        # raises if short -> no ledger row
516	5.   append two rows: (wallet, -amount), (EXTERNAL, +amount)
517	6. EXIT
518	7. return wallet.balance
519	```
520	🎙️ "Withdraw is one guarded debit. Even though it's single-wallet, it's still double-entry — the wallet is debited and an EXTERNAL sink is credited, so the ledger still sums to zero. Deposit is the mirror: credit the wallet, debit EXTERNAL."
521	
522	#### `_replay_if_seen` (the idempotency lookup)
523	**Pseudocode**
524	```
525	1. prior = _results_by_key.get(key)
526	2. if prior is None: return None
527	3. if prior params match (source, dest, amount): return prior
528	4. else: raise IdempotencyConflictError
529	```
530	🎙️ "This is an O(1) hit on the `_results_by_key` map. The subtle line is step 4 — a key reused for a *different* transfer is a client bug, not a retry, so I raise instead of silently returning the wrong receipt."
531	
532	#### `ledger_balance` (the conservation proof)
533	**Pseudocode**
534	```
535	1. return sum(entry.delta_cents for entry in _ledger)
536	```
537	🎙️ "This is the one-line proof the books balance. Because every move writes a + and a − of equal size, the sum is invariantly zero — I assert it after every demo case."
538	
539	### C. Data Structure Choices
540	
541	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
542	|---|---|---|---|
543	| `dict[str, Wallet]` | wallet registry | O(1) lookup by id; a list would be O(n) per transfer to find each wallet | O(1) get/set |
544	| `dict[str, TransferResult]` | idempotency store | retry must be O(1) — scanning a list of all transfers on every send would be O(n) in the hot path | O(1) |
545	| `list[LedgerEntry]` (append-only) | the double-entry ledger | appends are O(1) and order is the audit trail; a dict keyed by transfer id would lose insertion order and complicate the sum | O(1) append, O(n) sum |
546	| `int` cents | all money | exact arithmetic; floats lose fractions of a cent and the ledger stops summing to zero | O(1) |
547	| `defaultdict[str, RLock]` (subclass) | per-wallet locks | one lock per wallet → unrelated transfers run in parallel; a single global lock would serialize the whole system | O(1) |
548	
549	I chose a **list scan for `ledger_balance`** (O(n)) over keeping a running total because the sum is only computed in tests/audits, not the hot path. I'd switch to an incremental running total if `ledger_balance` were called per-request at scale.
550	
551	### D. Tricky Logic
552	
553	> **🧠 Claim:** A transfer must be all-or-nothing — the destination is never credited unless the source was successfully debited.
554	> **🔬 Proof:** If you credit first then debit, and the source is short, the destination keeps money that was never paid for — money created from nothing, ledger no longer sums to zero.
555	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `transfer` calls `source.debit(amount)` *before* `dest.credit(amount)`. `debit` raises `InsufficientFundsError` and nothing after it runs — no credit, no ledger rows, no recorded result.
556	
557	> **🧠 Claim:** A retried transfer with the same key must apply exactly once.
558	> **🔬 Proof:** Network drops, client re-sends. Without dedup, both calls debit the source twice.
559	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_replay_if_seen` consults `_results_by_key` *inside the lock*; the first call records the result at step 11, the retry returns it. `test_duplicate_key_applies_once` proves balances change once and only two ledger rows exist.
560	
561	> **🧠 Claim:** Two reciprocal transfers (A→B and B→A) must not deadlock.
562	> **🔬 Proof:** Thread 1 locks A then waits for B; thread 2 locks B then waits for A → both wait forever.
563	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ThreadSafeWalletService._lock_wallets` sorts `{first.id, second.id}` and acquires in that fixed order, so both threads grab A before B — no cycle. The concurrency test joins with a timeout that turns any deadlock into a failure.
564	
565	> **🧠 Claim:** The idempotency check must be inside the lock, not before it.
566	> **🔬 Proof:** If checked outside, two retries both read "not seen", both proceed, both move money.
567	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** the `_replay_if_seen` call sits *inside* `with self._lock_wallets(...)`, and the result is recorded inside the same critical section so it's published atomically with the money move.
568	
569	> **🧠 Claim:** Money must be integer cents, never float.
570	> **🔬 Proof:** `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3` in float; summing thousands of float deltas drifts off zero and the conservation check fails spuriously.
571	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** every amount is typed `int`; `delta_cents` is a signed int; the demo asserts `ledger_balance() == 0` exactly.
572	
573	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
574	
575	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
576	> ```
577	> def transfer(self, src, dst, amount):
578	>     self._wallets[src].balance_cents -= amount   # direct write, no guard
579	>     self._wallets[dst].balance_cents += amount   # no ledger, no idempotency
580	> ```
581	> **Why it's wrong:** three failure modes. (1) Direct balance writes bypass the no-negative rule — the source can go negative. (2) No ledger means you can't *prove* money was conserved, and a crash between the two lines destroys money. (3) No idempotency key means a client retry double-moves. The correct version asks the wallet to `debit`/`credit` (so it guards itself), writes two ledger rows via `_record_pair`, and records the outcome under an idempotency key.

749	
750	    def credit(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:

752	        if amount_cents <= 0:
753	            raise InvalidAmountError(f"credit amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")

758	        """Remove money, refusing to go negative. The core money invariant."""
759	        if amount_cents <= 0:

761

763	        #      wallet can debit itself, there is exactly ONE place this rule
764	        #      lives — it can never be bypassed by a service code path.
765	        if amount_cents > self.balance_cents:

815	class IdGenerator(Protocol):
816	    def next_id(self) -> str: ...
817

819	class SequentialIdGenerator:
820	    """Hands out transfer-1, transfer-2, … — readable and thread-safe."""
821	
822	    def __init__(self, prefix: str = "transfer") -> None:
823	        self._prefix = prefix
824	        self._counter = itertools.count(1)
825	        # WHY: a tiny lock makes next_id atomic so two threads never collide.
826	        self._lock = threading.Lock()
827	
828	    def next_id(self) -> str:
829	        with self._lock:
830	            n = next(self._counter)
831	
832	        return f"{self._prefix}-{n}"

867	#### `transfer` — the atomic, idempotent pipeline
868	
869	**1. Role:** the core money-movement workflow — it ties the idempotency check, the two-sided balance change, and the two ledger rows into one all-or-nothing step.
870	
871	**2. Core logic (happy path):**
872	1. Validate shape (distinct wallets, positive amount, both exist)
873	2. Lock both wallets in wallet-id order
874	3. If the key was already applied → return the recorded result
875	4. Debit the source (raises if short — nothing else runs)
876	5. Credit the destination
877	6. Append two ledger rows summing to zero
878	7. Record the result under the key and return it
879	
880	**3. Edge cases:** unknown wallet, self-transfer, non-positive amount, insufficient funds, key reused for a different transfer.
881	
882	**4. Code:**
883	
884	```python copy
885	def transfer(
886	    self,
887	    source_id: str,
888	    dest_id: str,
889	    amount_cents: int,
890	    idempotency_key: str,
891	) -> TransferResult:
892	    """Move money between two wallets — atomic and idempotent."""
893	    # WHY: validate the SHAPE of the request before touching any state.
894	    self._validate_transfer(source_id, dest_id, amount_cents)
895	    source = self._get(source_id)
896	    dest = self._get(dest_id)
897	
898	    with self._lock_wallets(source, dest):
899	        # WHY: idempotency check INSIDE the lock so two concurrent retries of
900	        #      the same key can't both pass and both apply.
901	        replay = self._replay_if_seen(idempotency_key, source_id, dest_id, amount_cents)
902	
903	        if replay is not None:
904	            return replay
905	
906	        transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
907	        # WHY: debit BEFORE credit. If the source is short, debit raises and
908	        #      nothing else happened — all-or-nothing.
909	        source.debit(amount_cents)
910	        dest.credit(amount_cents)
911	        self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id=source_id, dst_id=dest_id, amount=amount_cents)
912	
913	        result = TransferResult(
914	            transfer_id=transfer_id,
915	            idempotency_key=idempotency_key,
916	            source_wallet_id=source_id,
917	            dest_wallet_id=dest_id,
918	            amount_cents=amount_cents,
919	            source_balance_after=source.balance_cents,
920	            dest_balance_after=dest.balance_cents,
921	        )
922	        # WHY: publish the result under the key INSIDE the lock, atomically with
923	        #      the money move, so the next retry finds it.
924	        self._results_by_key[idempotency_key] = result
925	        return result
926	```
927	
928	**5. Reasoning:** This is the whole atomic story in one method. The `source.debit` call is the line that makes the transfer all-or-nothing — if it raises, the destination is never credited and no ledger rows are written, so the books stay balanced and the idempotency key is *not* recorded (so a corrected retry can still succeed). Notice we are NOT writing balances directly — we delegate to `debit`/`credit` so the wallet guards its own no-negative rule. The idempotency check and the result-publish both sit inside the lock so a retry that arrives between them can never double-apply. In production the ledger append would be a transactional write to an append-only store; here it's an in-memory list, which is the only interview-vs-production gap.
929	
930	**6. 🎙️ Out-loud + ⚠️ follow-up:**
931	> 🎙️ Say: "Validate, lock both in id-order, idempotency check inside the lock, debit-then-credit, two ledger rows, record the result. Debit before credit is what makes it all-or-nothing."
932	> ⚠️ "What if the same key is retried at the exact same instant?" → "Both serialize on the wallet locks. The first records the result; the second finds it inside the lock and returns it — money moves exactly once."
933	
934	##### Approach (the `_lock_wallets` ordering — the most interesting helper)
935	
936	**Approach 1 — lock in argument order (rejected):**
937	```
938	lock(source); lock(dest)
939	```
940	> **Challenges:** A→B locks source=A then dest=B; B→A locks source=B then dest=A. Two threads, opposite orders → each holds one lock and waits for the other → **deadlock**, the process hangs forever. I verified this exact hang on a deliberately-unordered variant of my own service.
941	
942	**Approach 2 — one global lock (rejected for this scale):**
943	```
944	with self._global_lock: ...  # every transfer serializes
945	```
946	> **Challenges:** Correct and deadlock-free, but it serializes the entire system — a transfer between C and D blocks an unrelated transfer between E and F. Throughput collapses under load. I'd only accept this if contention were trivially low. Flip to per-resource locking when transfers/sec crosses meaningful concurrency.
947	
948	**Chosen approach — per-wallet locks acquired in sorted id order:**
949	```
950	for wid in sorted({source.id, dest.id}): lock(wid)
951	```
952	> **Why it wins:** Unrelated transfers run fully in parallel (single source of truth per wallet), AND deadlock is impossible — every thread acquires A before B because both sort the same way, so no lock-ordering cycle can form.
953

957	def deposit(self, wallet_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> int:
958	    """Add external money into a wallet. Returns the new balance."""
959	    wallet = self._get(wallet_id)
960	
961	    with self._lock_wallets(wallet, wallet):
962	        transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
963	        wallet.credit(amount_cents)
964	        # WHY: even a deposit is double-entry — wallet CREDITED, EXTERNAL DEBITED.
965	        self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id="EXTERNAL", dst_id=wallet_id, amount=amount_cents)
966	
967	    return wallet.balance_cents
968	
969	
970	def withdraw(self, wallet_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> int:
971	    """Remove money out of a wallet to the external world. Returns balance."""
972	    wallet = self._get(wallet_id)
973	
974	    with self._lock_wallets(wallet, wallet):
975	        transfer_id = self._id_gen.next_id()
976	        # WHY: debit FIRST inside the guard — short funds raise, no ledger row.
977	        wallet.debit(amount_cents)
978	        self._record_pair(transfer_id, src_id=wallet_id, dst_id="EXTERNAL", amount=amount_cents)

1029

1037	def _replay_if_seen(self, idempotency_key, source_id, dest_id, amount_cents) -> TransferResult | None:
1038	    prior = self._results_by_key.get(idempotency_key)
1039	
1040	    if prior is None:
1041	        return None
1042	
1043	    same = (
1044	        prior.source_wallet_id == source_id
1045	        and prior.dest_wallet_id == dest_id
1046	        and prior.amount_cents == amount_cents
1047	    )
1048	
1049	    if not same:
1050	        raise IdempotencyConflictError(
1051	            f"idempotency key {idempotency_key} was used for a different transfer"
1052	        )
1053	
1054	    return prior

1058	    # WHY: the single place ledger rows are created — two rows, deltas sum to 0.
1059	    self._ledger.append(LedgerEntry(transfer_id, src_id, EntryType.DEBIT, -amount))
1060	    self._ledger.append(LedgerEntry(transfer_id, dst_id, EntryType.CREDIT, +amount))

1065	### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs
1066	
1067	**Scenario 1 — Happy path** (`alice=10000, bob=0`):
1068	Initial: `_ledger=[]`, `_results_by_key={}`
1069	
1070	| Step | Call | After |
1071	|---|---|---|
1072	| 1 | `transfer("alice","bob",3000,"t1")` | alice 7000, bob 3000; ledger `[(alice,-3000),(bob,+3000)]`; `_results_by_key={"t1": res}`; `ledger_balance()==0` ✓ |
1073	| 2 | `transfer("alice","bob",3000,"t1")` (retry) | **no change** — returns recorded `res`; ledger still 2 rows; `ledger_balance()==0` ✓ |
1074	
1075	**Scenario 2 — Insufficient funds is atomic** (`alice=500, bob=0`):
1076	
1077	| Step | Call | After |
1078	|---|---|---|
1079	| 1 | `transfer("alice","bob",999,"x")` | raises `InsufficientFundsError`; alice 500, bob 0; `_ledger=[]`; `_results_by_key={}` (key NOT recorded, so a corrected retry can still succeed) ✓ |
1080	
1081	**Scenario 3 — Race, in plain English:** Without the lock, two concurrent `transfer("alice","bob",...)` could both read alice's balance, both pass the implicit "enough?" check inside `debit`, and one of them could overdraw — or two retries of the same key could both miss the idempotency check and double-move. With the per-wallet locks acquired in id order, both serialize on alice's lock: the first commits and records; the second either sees the recorded key (retry) or sees the reduced balance (`InsufficientFundsError`). No overdraw, no double-apply, and because both threads always lock the lower id first, no deadlock.

1084	
1085	| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
1086	|---|---|---|---|
1087	| `create_wallet` | O(1) | O(1) | dict insert |

1182	**Which flow and why:** I'm showing `transfer` with a duplicate-key retry, because it touches every class — the service, both wallets, the ledger, the idempotency store — and shows exactly how atomicity and idempotency fire at runtime.
1183	
1184	```mermaid
1185	sequenceDiagram
1186	    actor Client
1187	    participant S as WalletService
1188	    participant Src as Wallet src
1189	    participant Dst as Wallet dst
1190	    Client->>S: transfer(src, dst, 3000, key t1)
1191	    S->>S: validate then lock both wallets in id order
1192	    S->>S: check idempotency store for key t1 (miss)
1193	    S->>Src: debit(3000)
1194	    Src-->>S: ok balance now 7000
1195	    S->>Dst: credit(3000)
1196	    Dst-->>S: ok balance now 3000
1197	    S->>S: append two ledger rows summing to zero
1198	    S->>S: record TransferResult under key t1
1199	    S-->>Client: TransferResult balances 7000 and 3000
1200	    Client->>S: transfer(src, dst, 3000, key t1) retry
1201	    S->>S: check idempotency store for key t1 (hit)
1202	    S-->>Client: same recorded TransferResult no money moved

1204	
1205	> 🎙️ **Script — arrow by arrow:** "The client calls `transfer` on the service. The service validates, then locks both wallets in id order — that's the deadlock-safe step. It checks the idempotency store; first time it's a miss. It calls `debit` on the source wallet — the wallet itself enforces the no-negative rule and drops to 7000. Then `credit` on the destination — 3000. The interesting moment is the next two self-calls: append two ledger rows that sum to zero, and record the result under key `t1`, both inside the lock so they're atomic with the money move. It returns the result. Then the client retries the same key — now the idempotency check is a hit, and the service returns the *same* recorded result without moving a cent."
1206	
1207	> **Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the service coordinates but never sets a balance itself — the wallets do, guarding their own rule. And the retry returning instantly from the store, with no debit/credit arrows at all, is idempotency made visible.

1210	
1211	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1212	
1213	**DEFAULT: describe verbally. Write the subclass only if asked.**
1214	
1215	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact races:** "There are two. First, `transfer` has a check-then-act on the balance — two concurrent transfers both read 'enough funds', both debit, the wallet overdraws. Second, two reciprocal transfers (A→B and B→A) lock two accounts, and if they grab them in opposite orders they deadlock."
1216	
1217	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:** "I give each wallet its own `RLock`. A transfer acquires the locks for *both* wallets before doing the check-and-move, and it acquires them in a fixed global order — sorted by wallet id. That makes the debit/credit/ledger/idempotency block one atomic step, and the ordered acquisition makes deadlock impossible because no two threads can hold locks in opposite orders."
1218	
1219	🗣️ **Step 3 — Trade-off:** "Per-wallet locks let unrelated transfers (C→D and E→F) run fully in parallel — only transfers touching the *same* wallet serialize. A single global lock would be simpler but would bottleneck the whole system."
1220	
1221	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case:** "Across servers an in-process lock is useless — each process has its own memory. I'd push atomicity to the database: a conditional `UPDATE wallets SET balance = balance - ? WHERE id = ? AND balance >= ?` (compare-and-set), wrap both legs in a DB transaction, and use a unique index on the idempotency key so a duplicate insert fails atomically."
1222	
1223	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**
1224	
1225	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1226	|---|---|---|
1227	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | balance check + debit in `transfer` |
1228	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the idempotency-check → debit → credit → ledger → record block |
1229	| **Check-then-act** | Check a condition, then act, but state changed in between | "saw enough funds, then debited" |
1230	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observed | what the per-wallet locks guarantee for the block |
1231	| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per wallet |
1232	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a transfer holding both locks re-enters helpers safely |
1233	| **Lock granularity** | Coarse (one global) vs fine (per-resource) | we chose per-WALLET |
1234	| **Lock ordering** | Always acquire multiple locks in the same order | sorted by wallet id — kills deadlock |
1235	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided by ordered acquisition |
1236	| **Idempotency** | Repeating an op has the same effect as doing it once | the `_results_by_key` store |
1237	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` approach |
1238	| **Optimistic / CAS** | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry if you lost | the distributed `UPDATE ... WHERE balance >= ?` |
1239	
1240	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**
1241	
1242	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1243	> "A transfer holds both wallet locks and then calls helpers (`_replay_if_seen`, `_record_pair`) that run inside the same critical section. If those — or a future refactor — re-acquire the same lock on the same thread, a plain `Lock` self-deadlocks. `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases when the outermost acquire exits."
1244	
1245	> ❓ "How exactly do you avoid deadlock with two locks?"
1246	> "Deadlock needs two threads acquiring locks in *opposite* orders. I `sorted({src.id, dst.id})` and acquire in that order, so A→B and B→A both grab A's lock before B's. No cycle can form. I verified the failure mode: a deliberately-unordered variant of my own service hangs under the exact test my ordered version passes."
1247	
1248	> ❓ "Why per-wallet locks instead of one global lock?"
1249	> "Throughput. A global lock serializes every transfer in the system even when they share no wallet. Per-wallet means C→D and E→F run in parallel; only transfers touching the same wallet wait. The cost is a tiny bit more locking machinery and the ordering discipline."
1250	
1251	> ❓ "Where does idempotency interact with the lock?"
1252	> "The check and the record both happen *inside* the lock. If they were outside, two concurrent retries could both miss the check and both apply. Inside the lock, the first records the result and the second returns it — exactly once."
1253	
1254	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1255	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny, so a short-held lock with no retry loop is simplest. Distributed, I flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set, because you can't hold a Python lock across servers, and a conditional write + retry is the idiomatic atomic primitive there."
1256	
1257	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version:**
1258	
1259	**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
1260	
1261	```python copy
1262	# ─── §6 core: the hook does nothing ────────────────────────────────────────
1263	@contextmanager
1264	def _lock_wallets(self, first: Wallet, second: Wallet) -> Iterator[None]:
1265	    yield                          # clean core: zero lock behavior
1266	
1267	# ─── §9 subclass: override ONLY the hook ────────────────────────────────────
1268	class ThreadSafeWalletService(WalletService):
1269	    def __init__(self, clock=None, id_gen=None) -> None:
1270	        super().__init__(clock=clock, id_gen=id_gen)
1271	        # one RLock per wallet id, created on demand
1272	        self._wallet_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = defaultdict(threading.RLock)
1273	        self._locks_guard = threading.Lock()     # guards the dict of locks itself
1274	        self._registry_lock = threading.RLock()  # guards ledger + idempotency store
1275	
1276	    def _lock_for(self, wallet_id: str) -> threading.RLock:
1277	        # lock the dict FIRST, then fetch the per-wallet lock
1278	        with self._locks_guard:
1279	            return self._wallet_locks[wallet_id]
1280	
1281	    @contextmanager
1282	    def _lock_wallets(self, first: Wallet, second: Wallet) -> Iterator[None]:
1283	        ids = sorted({first.wallet_id, second.wallet_id})   # ← fixed global order
1284	        locks = [self._lock_for(wid) for wid in ids]
1285	        acquired: list[threading.RLock] = []
1286	        try:
1287	            for lock in locks:
1288	                lock.acquire()
1289	                acquired.append(lock)
1290	            with self._registry_lock:           # ledger + idempotency atomic too
1291	                yield
1292	        finally:
1293	            for lock in reversed(acquired):     # release on EVERY path
1294	                lock.release()
1295	```
1296

The hook acquires per-wallet RLocks in sorted id order (deadlock-free) and wraps the ledger/idempotency writes in the registry lock; everything releases in `finally` so a failed transfer never leaks a lock."

# Build a DELIBERATELY-UNORDERED variant: lock in argument order (not sorted) with
# a sleep between the two acquires to force the classic A->B / B->A deadlock.
import time, threading as th
class BadOrderService(ThreadSafeWalletService):
    @contextmanager
    def _lock_wallets(self, first, second):
        l1=self._lock_for(first.wallet_id)
        l2=self._lock_for(second.wallet_id)
        l1.acquire()
        time.sleep(0.01)
        l2.acquire()
        try:
            with self._registry_lock:
                yield
        finally:
            l2.release(); l1.release()
